application_details.repayment_terms: Requested 10-year term (120 months) with fixed monthly principal and interest payments, amortized over the full term, with no balloon payment. Proposed automatic ACH debit on the 5th of each month from the business operating account at Bank of Guam. No interest-only period requested; principal repayment to begin with the first scheduled payment 30 days after initial disbursement.

business_information.business_physical_address: 145 Marine Corps Drive, Suite 204, Hagåtña, GU 96910
business_information.briefly_describe_business_activity:
Island Harvest Foods, LLC (dba Island Harvest Kitchen) is a small-scale food manufacturing and catering company based in Hagåtña, Guam that focuses on producing value-added products from locally sourced agricultural ingredients and providing catering services tailored to both residents and visitors. We partner directly with local farmers in Dededo, Yigo, and Inarajan to purchase fresh produce such as bananas, breadfruit, taro, coconut, and local greens, which we then process into shelf-stable items like jams, chutneys, pickled vegetables, and frozen ready-to-heat meals featuring Chamorro and Pacific fusion recipes.

Our core business lines are: (1) light manufacturing of packaged food products (jars of coconut-lime jam, breadfruit chips, banana bread loaves, and vacuum-sealed marinated meats) sold wholesale to grocery stores, hotel gift shops, and convenience stores; (2) catering and corporate lunch services for local businesses, government offices, and community events; and (3) tourism-oriented food experiences, including small-group cooking classes for hotel guests, curated tasting platters for tour operators, and packaged snack boxes highlighting local flavors for airlines and travel agencies.

We currently operate out of a leased commercial kitchen facility that meets Guam Department of Public Health and Social Services requirements, and we have invested in small-batch equipment such as convection ovens, a commercial mixer, vacuum sealers, and food dehydrators. Our products are branded under the Island Harvest Kitchen label with packaging that emphasizes Guam-grown ingredients and stories about the local farmers we work with. We also maintain a modest online presence through our website and social media channels, where we take pre-orders for holiday gift baskets and corporate orders. The business is in a growth phase, with demand from hotels and retail outlets outpacing our current production capacity, which is a primary driver for this loan request.

repayment_information.repayment_manner_description:
The loan will be repaid through regular, fixed monthly payments of principal and interest over a proposed 10-year (120-month) term. Based on a requested principal amount of $300,000 and assuming a competitive fixed interest rate in line with current GEDA/GDFA programs, our internal projections estimate a monthly payment in the range of $3,200–$3,600. We plan to set up an automatic ACH debit from our primary operating account at Bank of Guam, scheduled for the 5th of each month to align with our cash inflows from wholesale customers and catering contracts.

We anticipate that the loan will be fully amortizing, with no balloon payment at maturity. We are not requesting an interest-only period; instead, we prefer to begin reducing principal immediately, with the understanding that our expanded production capacity and sales contracts will support the required payments. In the first 12–18 months, we expect a ramp-up period as new equipment is installed and new accounts are onboarded; to address this, we have built conservative cash flow projections that assume only partial utilization of capacity in year one and gradually increasing volumes thereafter.

Our internal policy will be to maintain a minimum cash reserve equal to at least three months of loan payments in a separate savings account. We will review our cash position weekly and, when possible, prepay small additional amounts of principal during stronger sales months (for example, during the holiday season when gift basket sales spike) to reduce overall interest costs and build additional safety margin into our repayment schedule.
repayment_information.source_of_repayment:
The primary source of repayment will be operating cash flow generated by Island Harvest Foods, LLC from three main revenue streams: (1) wholesale sales of our packaged food products to supermarkets, convenience stores, and hotel gift shops; (2) catering and corporate meal contracts with local businesses and government agencies; and (3) tourism-related food experiences and packaged gift sets for visitors.

1. Wholesale Packaged Products: We currently have standing purchase orders with two major grocery chains on Guam, along with smaller independent retailers. With the additional equipment financed by this loan (larger convection ovens, a commercial-scale dehydrator, expanded cold storage, and improved packaging machinery), we project a 60–80% increase in monthly production capacity within the first 18 months. Conservatively, we forecast wholesale revenues growing from approximately $32,000 per month currently to $55,000–$60,000 per month by year three. These revenues are relatively stable and recurring, as our products occupy shelf space under long-term vendor relationships.

2. Catering and Corporate Meal Services: We provide catered lunches and event catering for several offices in Hagåtña and Tamuning, as well as for community events and family gatherings. We have signed annual agreements (renewable) with two mid-sized employers to provide weekly staff lunches, which alone generate around $8,000–$10,000 per month. With the expanded kitchen and improved equipment, we will be able to take on additional contracts that we currently turn down due to capacity limits. We project catering-related revenues growing from roughly $15,000 per month to $25,000–$30,000 per month over the next two years.

3. Tourism-Oriented Services and Gift Products: We currently work with two hotel partners in Tumon to provide locally themed welcome snack boxes for VIP guests and small-group cooking classes featuring Chamorro recipes. This segment is smaller but high-margin. With a more efficient production flow and dedicated packaging area funded by this loan, we expect to scale this line modestly from about $6,000 per month to $12,000–$15,000 per month, depending on seasonal tourism patterns.

In addition to these ongoing revenue sources, we have a signed letter of intent from a regional distributor in the Northern Mariana Islands to carry our top-selling coconut-lime jam and breadfruit chips in Saipan and Tinian, contingent on our ability to meet minimum monthly volume requirements. This expansion is built into our year-two and year-three projections but not assumed in the first-year cash flow, providing upside potential without relying on it for initial repayment capacity.

Secondary sources of repayment include:
- The personal salary deferral commitments of the managing member (Maria Elena Santos) and the minority individual member (Daniel Robert Cruz) during the first 12–18 months if needed. Both have agreed, in writing, to cap their salary draws and temporarily reduce personal compensation to maintain adequate debt service coverage.
- A modest revolving line of credit with Bank of Guam (currently $50,000, of which we typically use less than 30%) that can provide short-term working capital support if there are timing gaps between large inventory purchases and receivable collections.

We have prepared detailed monthly cash flow projections that show a minimum debt service coverage ratio (DSCR) of 1.35x in year one, increasing to over 1.75x by year three, even under conservative sales assumptions. We collect most receivables from wholesale customers within 30 days, and catering clients typically pay a 50% deposit upfront, which further stabilizes our cash position. These combined revenue streams and financial safeguards will be used to make the scheduled loan payments reliably over the life of the loan.
